column2: {p} 9*A. EMILY* │ In my mid-twenties 8*I joined the staff* of a language school. The pay wasn’t brilliant but I could 8*make a living* and there were many 8*aspects of the job* that I enjoyed. The other 8*members of staff* were nice and I enjoyed teaching the students. A few years later
column4: * I decided to 8*go part-time*. Luckily I was able to 8*do a job-share* with another woman who had a small child. Then the school began to go through a difficult period and had 8*to lay off staff*. I decided to *go freelance*. I had managed to build up a *network of contacts* and this gave me a good start. I soon had a substantial *volume of work* - private students and marking exams - and was able to 8*earn a good living*.❗9*B. BEN* │ After graduating
column5: I 8*practiced medicine* for a number of years in London. I managed to 8*carve a niche for myself* as a specialist in dermatology. Then I realized I needed some fresh challenges and so I did a job swap for a year with my 8*opposite number* in a clinic in Vancouver. When I returned
column6: I went back to my old job and also *took up* the *post* of editor of a leading medical journal. I 8*held that position* for a number of years. I’m now hoping to go abroad again and so am letting everyone know that I 8*am open to offers.*
